questions: An early-2000s introductory UNIX/Linux tutorial written by a UK-based academic and made freely available online states that it is distributed under an older Creative Commons license version, and it links to a canonical URL ending in “/2.0/”. Identify the exact Creative Commons license (full name and version) applied to this tutorial. Then, based on the official license deed, explain in detail: (1) the freedoms it grants users, (2) the specific conditions users must follow (including attribution, commercial use, and derivative works), (3) any stated limitations or disclaimers (such as warranties or additional rights), and (4) the notice regarding the recommended newer 4.0 version of the license.

A well-known early-2000s beginner tutorial on the UNIX/Linux operating system is distributed under an older Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike license. The license page itself recommends using a newer, more internationally robust 4.0 version instead. Identify the full name of this recommended 4.0 license and provide its canonical URL. Then, based on the official license deed, comprehensively describe: (1) the freedoms granted to users, (2) all core conditions imposed on reuse and redistribution, and (3) the key legal notices and disclaimers regarding warranties, legal advice, and the status of the deed itself.

rounds.question: An early-2000s introductory UNIX/Linux tutorial written by a UK-based academic and made freely available online states that it is distributed under an older Creative Commons license version, and it links to a canonical URL ending in “/2.0/”. Identify the exact Creative Commons license (full name and version) applied to this tutorial. Then, based on the official license deed, explain in detail: (1) the freedoms it grants users, (2) the specific conditions users must follow (including attribution, commercial use, and derivative works), (3) any stated limitations or disclaimers (such as warranties or additional rights), and (4) the notice regarding the recommended newer 4.0 version of the license.
checklist_width: Correct identification of the freedoms: Share (copy and redistribute) and Adapt (remix, transform, build upon)
Statement that freedoms cannot be revoked if license terms are followed
Attribution requirement (credit, link to license, indicate changes, no implied endorsement)
ShareAlike requirement (derivatives must use the same license)
No warranties disclaimer and note about other rights (e.g., privacy, publicity, moral rights)
Reference to recommendation of the newer 4.0 version as more user-friendly and internationally robust
checklist_depth: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 + Identified via canonical URL ending in /2.0/
Recognition that this is an older license version with an official notice recommending the 4.0 version
rounds.rationale: The question uses contextual clues (early-2000s UNIX/Linux tutorial by a UK academic with a CC license URL ending in /2.0/) to require logical identification of the specific Creative Commons license version (Deep reasoning). It then requires comprehensive synthesis of multiple sections of the official license deed—freedoms, conditions, disclaimers, and upgrade notice—ensuring broad aggregation of details from the license text itself (Wide scope).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Notice This is an older version of this license. Compared to previous versions, the 4.0 versions of all CC licenses are more user-friendly and more internationally robust . If
rounds.answer_a:
The tutorial is **"UNIX Tutorial for Beginners"** by Michael Stonebank at the University of Surrey, UK. It is licensed under the **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 License** (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0), with the canonical deed URL ending in `/2.0/` [1][2].

**(1) Freedoms Granted:**
The license grants users two core freedoms. First, users may **copy, distribute, display, and perform** the work. Second, users may **create derivative works** (adaptations, remixes, transformations) based on the original material. These freedoms apply worldwide and are granted free of charge [3].

**(2) Conditions Users Must Follow:**
Three specific conditions apply. **Attribution (BY):** Users must give appropriate credit to the original creator, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made, in a reasonable manner. **NonCommercial (NC):** Users may not use the work for **commercial purposes**—the material cannot be used primarily for commercial advantage or monetary compensation. **ShareAlike (SA):** If users remix, transform, or build upon the material, they must distribute their contributions under the **same or compatible license** as the original [3].

**(3) Limitations and Disclaimers:**
The license explicitly states it does **not** affect fair dealing/fair use rights or other applicable exceptions. It provides **no warranties**—the work is offered "as-is." The licensor is not required to grant additional permissions beyond those stated. Moral rights and rights of other persons (e.g., publicity, privacy) may still limit use [3].

**(4) Notice Regarding the Newer 4.0 Version:**
The official CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 deed now displays a prominent notice informing users that this is **not the latest version** and recommending they consider using **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International** for their own works, which is the most current and internationally standardized version of this license [3].

## References
[1] Stonebank, M. "UNIX Tutorial for Beginners." University of Surrey. http://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/
[2] Creative Commons License link from tutorial: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/
[3] Creative Commons. "Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 Generic (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0)." https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/
rounds.answer_b:
The tutorial "UNIX Tutorial for Beginners" by M. Stonebank of the University of Surrey is distributed under the **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales** license [1, 2].

(1) This license grants users the **freedom** to Share (copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format) and Adapt (remix, transform, and build upon the material) [2].

(2) Users must follow several **conditions**. First is **Attribution**, requiring they give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate if any changes were made. This must be done reasonably but not in a way that suggests the licensor endorses the new work. Second, the **NonCommercial** clause prohibits using the material for commercial purposes. Third, the **ShareAlike** condition mandates that if you remix, transform, or build upon the material, you must distribute your contributions under the very same license as the original [2].

(3) The license includes important **limitations and disclaimers**. It comes with no warranties, and the licensor provides the work "as-is" without assurances of any kind. This means the licensor is not liable for damages arising from its use. The license does not limit a user's rights from exceptions and limitations to copyright, such as fair dealing or fair use [2].

(4) The official Creative Commons deed for this 2.0 license contains a prominent **notice regarding newer versions**. It states that this is a legacy version and recommends against using it for new works. Instead, Creative Commons encourages creators to apply the more globally robust and user-friendly Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International license for new projects [2, 3].

## References
[1] Stonebank, M. (n.d.). *UNIX Tutorial for Beginners: Copyright*. University of Surrey. Retrieved from http://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/copyright.html
[2] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK)*. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/
[3] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Creative Commons — Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International — CC BY-NC-SA 4.0*. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A identifies the license as Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 (generic) with a canonical URL ending in /2.0/, which matches the constraint. Agent B instead specifies the 2.0 UK: England & Wales port (/2.0/uk/), which does not align with the canonical /2.0/ requirement. Therefore, B has a Deep Logic failure (wrong specific license variant). 

Next, Width/Completeness: Both agents correctly describe the core freedoms (Share, Adapt), attribution, NonCommercial, ShareAlike, fair use exceptions, no warranties, and the 4.0 recommendation. However, both omit explicit mention of the “no additional restrictions” clause and do not clearly state that freedoms are irrevocable provided terms are followed. So both are slightly incomplete on the WIDTH checklist.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Both are well-structured and readable. Agent A has slightly clearer section formatting and more precise alignment with the generic license deed. Since A is accurate on the core entity and B is not, A wins. However, because A also misses minor checklist elements, this is [[A_BETTER]] rather than MUCH_BETTER.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: DEEP.

rounds.question: A well-known early-2000s beginner tutorial on the UNIX/Linux operating system is distributed under an older Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike license. The license page itself recommends using a newer, more internationally robust 4.0 version instead. Identify the full name of this recommended 4.0 license and provide its canonical URL. Then, based on the official license deed, comprehensively describe: (1) the freedoms granted to users, (2) all core conditions imposed on reuse and redistribution, and (3) the key legal notices and disclaimers regarding warranties, legal advice, and the status of the deed itself.
checklist_width: Freedom to Share — copy and redistribute in any medium or format
Freedom to Adapt — remix, transform, and build upon the material
Condition: Attribution (credit, link to license, indicate changes, no endorsement implication)
Condition: ShareAlike (distribute contributions under the same license)
Condition: No additional restrictions (no legal/technological restrictions beyond license)
Statement that no warranties are given
Notice that the deed is not a license and has no legal value
Statement that Creative Commons is not a law firm and provides no legal services
checklist_depth: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International + Identified as the recommended upgrade over the older BY-NC-SA 2.0 license used by the UNIX/Linux beginner tutorial

Correct logical connection: The tutorial is under an older CC BY-NC-SA license, and the license notice explicitly recommends adopting the newer 4.0 version as more user-friendly and internationally robust
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep reasoning by requiring identification of the specific Creative Commons license recommended as an upgrade from the older version used by a UNIX/Linux beginner tutorial, without naming the document itself. It uses wide aggregation by demanding multiple categories of information from the official 4.0 license deed: canonical URL, user freedoms, all reuse conditions, and several distinct legal disclaimers. Answering fully requires synthesizing details from across the license summary, not just identifying its name.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Canonical URL https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/ See the legal code You are free to: Share — copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format Adapt —
rounds.answer_a:
The recommended newer license is the **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International** (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0). Its canonical URL is: **https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/** [1].

## (1) Freedoms Granted

The CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license grants users two fundamental freedoms [1][2]:

- **Share:** Users may copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format.
- **Adapt:** Users may remix, transform, and build upon the material.

These freedoms are granted for any purpose that is not primarily commercial. The licensor cannot revoke these freedoms as long as the license terms are followed. The license is worldwide, royalty-free, non-sublicensable, non-exclusive, and irrevocable [1][2].

## (2) Core Conditions

Three binding conditions govern reuse and redistribution [1][2]:

- **Attribution (BY):** Users must give appropriate credit to the creator, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made. This may be done in any reasonable manner, but not in a way suggesting the licensor endorses the user or their use.
- **NonCommercial (NC):** The material may not be used for commercial purposes—defined as use primarily intended for or directed toward commercial advantage or monetary compensation.
- **ShareAlike (SA):** If users remix, transform, or build upon the material, they must distribute their contributions under the same license (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0) or a compatible license.

## (3) Key Legal Notices and Disclaimers

The deed includes several important notices [1][2]:

- **No Warranties:** The license is provided "as-is." The licensor makes no warranties or guarantees regarding the work.
- **Not Legal Advice:** The deed is a human-readable summary of the full legal code and is not itself a legal instrument nor a substitute for legal counsel.
- **Additional Rights Unaffected:** The license does not reduce or limit fair use, fair dealing, or other exceptions. Moral rights, publicity, and privacy rights may still restrict how the material is used.
- **Deed Status:** The deed highlights that users should review the full legal code for binding terms, as the summary may not capture all nuances.

## References
[1] Creative Commons. "Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)." https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
[2] Creative Commons. "CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 Legal Code." https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/legalcode
rounds.answer_b:
The recommended newer license is the **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International**, with the canonical URL: `https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/` [1].

**(1) Freedoms Granted:** This license grants users two primary freedoms. The first is the freedom to **Share**, which allows anyone to copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format for noncommercial purposes. The second is the freedom to **Adapt**, permitting users to remix, transform, and build upon the material, again for noncommercial purposes [1]. These freedoms empower broad reuse and creative development.

**(2) Core Conditions:** To exercise these freedoms, users must adhere to three conditions. **Attribution (BY)** requires users to give appropriate credit to the original creator, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made. This must be done reasonably but not in a way that suggests the licensor endorses the user or their use. The **NonCommercial (NC)** term strictly prohibits using the material for commercial purposes. Finally, the **ShareAlike (SA)** condition mandates that if a user remixes, transforms, or builds upon the material, they must distribute their new creations under the identical CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 International license [1].

**(3) Legal Notices and Disclaimers:** The official deed includes several key disclaimers. It explicitly states there are **no warranties** given with the material; the license provides the work "as-is" and "as-available" and disclaims responsibility for any damages resulting from use. It also clarifies that the user-friendly deed is not the full license but a summary of its key terms, with a link provided to the complete Legal Code. A final notice clarifies that Creative Commons is not a law firm and does not provide legal advice; the information is for informational purposes only [1].

## References
[1] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)*. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the main entity: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International and provided the correct canonical URL. Thus, both pass the DEEP logic check. In terms of WIDTH, both covered the core freedoms (Share, Adapt), the primary conditions (Attribution, NonCommercial, ShareAlike), and key disclaimers (no warranties, deed not legal advice, CC not a law firm—explicit in B, implicit in A). However, both omitted explicit mention of the 'No additional restrictions' clause, so neither is perfectly complete. 

Comparing user experience and presentation, Agent A provides a more comprehensive and structured response. It includes clearer sectioning, slightly richer legal nuance (irrevocability, worldwide scope, fair use preservation), and more citation grounding (2 vs 1). The formatting is equally strong in both, but A delivers greater informational density and context while remaining within the word constraint. 

Therefore, Agent A is better on completeness and overall search-style depth, though both are factually accurate. The loser (B) did not fail on entity or major facts—so the failure type is NONE; A wins on superior comprehensiveness and utility.
